stations.label: THE URV ILLUSION
stations.line: Meu Deus do céu, they invented a fake currency first — the URV, a unit of value nobody could spend, just to teach a whole country how to trust prices again, and only when we all believed did I become real on the first of July; tá ligado, I was born from a trick that worked.
stations.era_photo: A young woman standing outside a São Paulo bank branch in July 1994, wearing a floral rayon blouse and high-waisted jeans, holding crisp new banknotes to the camera; grainy 35mm color print with the slightly washed, warm tones of early-90s Brazilian photojournalism.

stations.label: THE PEG BREAKS FREE
stations.line: For five years they held me tight against the dollar like an anxious mother holding a child's hand too hard, and then in January they just — let go, and I dropped like a stone, and everyone screamed, but honestly? Vai dar certo, floating was the only way I ever learned to breathe.
stations.era_photo: The same woman mid-fall, arms out, on a trading floor in Rio in early 1999, wearing a fitted blazer with 90s shoulders and a worried face lit by monitor glow; early digital camera capture, low resolution, slight motion blur, cold fluorescent light.

stations.label: THE FEAR OF LULA
stations.line: The gringos looked at Lula, a metalworker with a beard, and they ran — ish — they pulled everything out and I collapsed to almost four to the dollar, all because they were scared of a man who turned out to love me more than any of them; that's my wound, tá ligado, they always leave right when I need them.
stations.era_photo: A woman clutching her chest at a currency exchange window in late 2002, wearing a plain dark cardigan, dark circles under her eyes, election posters faded behind her; muddy consumer digital photo, harsh on-camera flash, greenish shadows.

stations.label: THE GLOBAL SUDDEN STOP
stations.line: I had gotten strong, I was flying, close to one-and-a-half to the dollar and feeling gorgeous, and then Wall Street set fire to itself and the whole world grabbed their dollars and fled — abandonment again, always the same story, they love me until they're afraid, and then poof, saudade.
stations.era_photo: A well-dressed woman in a sharp 2008 pantsuit standing alone in an emptying financial district at dusk in São Paulo, phone pressed to ear; crisp compact-digital color, slightly oversharpened, bluish twilight tones.

stations.label: THE COMMODITY HANGOVER
stations.line: The party ended, my love — the China boom that fed me, the impeachment, the corruption everyone finally saw on television, and I sank past four to the dollar while the whole country argued at the dinner table; my budget was beautiful on Monday and dead by Wednesday, tá ligado.
stations.era_photo: A tired woman at a cluttered kitchen table covered in bills and a muted TV showing protests, wearing an old Brazil football shirt, 2015; warm smartphone photo, natural window light, honest and slightly grainy.

stations.label: THE PANDEMIC PLUNGE
stations.line: Meu Deus, the virus came and the world dumped everything emerging-market like it was contaminated, and I crashed past five, then near six to the dollar — my worst ever — but we did what we always do, we made caipirinha with the lemons life threw and whispered vai dar certo through our masks.
stations.era_photo: A masked woman at a shuttered street market in Rio, spring 2020, wearing a simple shirt and a cloth mask, empty stalls behind her; high-resolution phone photo, flat overcast light, muted colors, faint reflections.

stations.label: THE HIGH-RATE EXILE
stations.line: They tell me I'm 'undervalued,' that fiscal worries chase the money away again — six to the dollar, ish — and even with the highest real yields dangling like sweets, still they hesitate; you understand now, deixa eu resumir: my whole life is people deciding whether to stay.
stations.era_photo: A woman in her fifties, elegant and a little weary, on a modern glass balcony over a lit São Paulo skyline at night, wrapped in a shawl; clean high-end smartphone night mode, deep blacks, warm city bokeh.

stations.label: STILL HERE, STILL DANCING
stations.line: Thirty-one years old today and still standing — battered, floating, laughing from the belly, holding my contradictions like family; I never stopped being afraid the chaos returns, but honestly, meu amor, the fact that I'm still here at all is its own kind of axé.
stations.era_photo: A woman today on a warm balcony with fresh coffee and a laptop showing charts, laughing at something off-camera, colorful and relaxed; contemporary mirrorless portrait, soft golden-hour light, rich natural color.
